commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: 12. "Let the heavens rejoice, and let the earth be glad" (ver. 11). Let the heavens, which declare the glory of God, rejoice; let the heavens rejoice, which the Lord made; let the earth be glad, which the heavens rain upon. For the heavens are the preachers, the earth the listeners. "Let the sea be stirred up, and the fulness thereof." What sea? The world. The sea hath been stirred up, and the fulness thereof: the whole world was roused up against the Church, while it was being extended and built over all the earth. Concerning this stirring up, ye have heard in the Gospel, "They shall deliver you up to councils." "The sea was stirred up: but how should the sea ever conquer Him who made it?

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content:
Fulness. Its raging billows, (Calmet) fishes, (Menochius) those who live upon the water. (Haydock)
Let al testify their joy. Every thing is animated by the psalmist. (Calmet)

commentaries.author: Hippolytus of Rome
commentaries.content: 1 Esdras Wisdom of Solomon Wisdom of Sirach Epistle of Jeremiah Prayer of Manasseh Let the heavens rejoice, and let the earth be glad; let the sea roar, and the fullness thereof. Hippolytus of Rome “Let the Sea Roar (Be Moved), and the Fulness Thereof. ” By these words it is signified that the preaching of the Gospel will be spread abroad over the seas and the islands in the ocean, and among the people dwelling therein, who are here called “the fulness thereof. ” And that word has been made good. For churches of Christ fill all the islands, and are being multiplied every day, and the teaching of the Word of salvation is gaining accessions.
